title: Coming Home: The LC-3
lesson:
Time machine jumps to your own classroom. This is the LC-3, the teaching computer designed by Professor Yale Patt right here at UT Austin (with Sanjay Patel) for the textbook 'Introduction to Computing Systems'. It's the ISA you study in EE 306. It never existed as physical hardware. It's an idealized machine, built purely so students can learn how every computer really works.

And look how far you've come. The Manchester Baby had one register. The PDP-8 had one accumulator plus a Link bit. The 6502 had three. The MIPS chip inside the Nintendo 64 had 32. The LC-3 trims that back down to eight (R0 through R7, each 16 bits wide), not because more is bad, but because the whole point is to fit on one page so you can hold the entire machine in your head at once.

ADD works two ways: add two registers (ADD R0, R1, R2) or add a small constant (ADD R0, R1, #5), exactly like MIPS's add and addi. Every instruction that writes a register also sets the condition codes N, Z, and P, the same N/Z idea you saw in ARM's NZCV. You'll use those next.
prompt: Put 5 in R1, 7 in R2, then add them into R0 (so R0 ends up at 12). End with HALT. Programs begin with .ORIG x3000 and end with .END.
starterCode:
.ORIG x3000
        ADD R1, R1, #5   ; R1 = 0 + 5
        ADD R2, R2, #7   ; R2 = 0 + 7
        ADD R0, R1, R2   ; R0 = R1 + R2
        HALT
.END
hints: Registers start at 0, so ADD R1, R1, #5 leaves 5 in R1.
ADD R0, R1, R2 stores R1 plus R2 into R0.
successText: R0 must equal 12 (that's 5 plus 7) at the halt.
reference:
.ORIG x3000
ADD R1, R1, #5
ADD R2, R2, #7
ADD R0, R1, R2
HALT
.END
